We should expose the `--filter` parameter for `ls`, as it will give users much richer capabilities including glob-like functionality.

Reference for the API's filter param: https://docs.globus.org/api/transfer/file_operations/#list_directory_contents

I think it may be best to simply expose this parameter as a single option which directly passes values through. That probably means that we need to replicate the documentation for `filter` from the API, in the form of CLI doc.
Also, I think an example of simulating `ls | grep` with `globus ls --filter` would be nice.

I've been writing instructions for users to download the globuspersonalconnect client to create an endpoint for a specific folder, and I think this is reasonable given that they need to open up the browser and log in, etc. However I'm looking for a solution that is many fewer steps than what I'm figuring out to do. I am doing the following:

 - define computer as personal endpoint
 - edit config-paths to limit permissions 
 - edit (something else I haven't found yet) to define a shared folder to be where the images are.

I felt ok about 3 steps, but when I did a list (and then went to the web interface) I saw...

![image](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/814322/34441695-2f6aabc2-ec72-11e7-8d1f-ce943a4004a2.png)

Absolutely nothing! The reason of course being because the folder is hidden and I'd need to select to show it:

![image](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/814322/34441704-4b367a7a-ec72-11e7-86ec-d9aeff2f78f8.png)

But I think we can do better than this, because each step that we add on to the flow the more likely it is that the user is going to miss something and find it hard to use. Here is sort of what I'd like to do (and please let me know if this is reasonable).

## Endpoint Setup
The user has some (short) series of steps to set up the endpoint locally. Ideally, we could do this completely programatically (from within python) without needing to install "globuspersonalconnect" and "globus-cli" and still "globus-python-sdk" - that's a ton of dependencies for just this one thing! If I implemented it with the sregistry client, the logic would work as follows:

  1. The user creates the endpoint (or has sregistry do it for him/her) and adds the id to a variable that sregistry can access.
  2. When the client starts, the endpoint is found via the variable, and then the client finishes setting it up. This means **1)** creating a specific share for the sregistry storage folder (if it doesn't exist) and **2)** ensuring that the shared folder is by default not hidden (because by default it is hidden, and if I do a list on my command line I see nothing akin to the web interface.

This series of steps to read in the endpoint id and then check for paths and sharing should be the minimal requirement for setup. You can imagine if something is off the user can be notified immediately, and the functions that the user is given can be determined based on what is found.

Could you point me to the right docs / or make a suggestion about how to best accomplish this? I poked through the python sdk and the online docs but primarily see stuffs related to clients for transfer / server endpoints (as opposed to set up stuffs). Thank you!

issue_title: Make all components to work without `Vue.config.replace = false`
issue_body:
## Detailed Description
Vue 0.12 had a different behavior when instanciating components, it wasn't replacing the root element.
When migrating to Vue 1.x, we had to toggle `Vue.config.replace = false` in order to maintain a compatibility with some component.
This setting setting is deprecated and then removed in Vue 2.0.

## Context
In order to stay close to standard Vue.js behavior, to allow upgrade and to simplify component styling (the component will be exactly as written, no custom surronding element), we need to drop `Vue.config.replace = false` and fix components for this behavior.

issue_title: [gtk3] "ls -l" output underlined, should disallow space in filename
issue_body:
(Even with Quick Open disabled)

Execute `ls -l` and mouseover an entry that is newer than half year (showing hh:mm instead of year), e.g.

    drwxrwxrwt 16 root root 20480 Jan 25 01:19 tmp

Notice that up to (including) the timestamp it's all underlined.

I believe that the second regex of Quick Open assumes that this is a file named "drwxrwxrwt 16 root root 20480 Jan 25 01", and line 19.

Ctrl+click also prints silly things.

I think an easy and nice workaround could be to remove space from the allowed characters of filenames.

issue_title: Ctrl+D in the terminal does not close the tab anymore, but freezes the current tab
issue_body:
## Version information
**Guake version:** 3.0.4
**Linux version:** Debian buster
**Gnome version:** GNOME Shell 3.26.2

## Expected behavior:
When in a terminal, using Ctrl+D on an empty prompt exits the current terminal.
In previous versions of Guake, this would act as if the tab was closed: if it's not the last tab, only this tab is closed, if it's the last tab, the full Guake window would hide until the next F12 key press.

## Current bug
When using Ctrl+D on an empty prompt, the current tab freezes after printing "exit" on the terminal. The context menu can be opened on a right click, but selecting any option does not do anything. If another tab is opened, it can be used to right click but not all options work: 'New tab' works, 'Quit' does not work (probably blocked by the other tabs). Only solution to get back full control of Guake is to `killall guake`.

issue_title: Add ability to validate/document content of Response instances.
issue_body:
If your logic function returns a `doctor.response.Response` instance in order to set response headers or modify the status code, it's response will not be validated or documented, even if it's a doctor type.

It would be nice to add a Generic to the `Response` type so that the user could specify the type of the content of the Response.  e.g.

```python
from doctor.response import Response
from api import types as t
from api.model import Foo

def get_foo(foo_id: t.FooID) -> Response:
    foo = Foo.get_by_id(foo_id)
    return Response(foo, headers={'X-Custom': 'custom'})
```

This logic had to add a custom header to the response `X-Custom`.  Because of that it will not validate that the response is a valid `t.Foo`, nor will it document the response object.  If we add some code and make Response a `Generic`, we can make this possible.

```python
from doctor.response import Response
from api import types as t
from api.model import Foo

def get_foo(foo_id: t.FooID) -> Response[t.Foo]:
    foo = Foo.get_by_id(foo_id)
    return Response(foo, headers={'X-Custom': 'custom'})
```

https://docs.python.org/3/library/typing.html#user-defined-generic-types

issue_title: list_objects() and list_objects_v2() methods fail when executed on GCS
issue_body:
I have a two object storage environments setup. One with the Minio Server and the other one on GCS.
Both environments have the exact same folder structure and when migrating from Minio server to GCS the list object functionality started returning errors.

```
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "/home/zupan/bin/miniconda/lib/python3.6/site-packages/minio/parsers.py", line 108, in get_child_text
    return self.element.find('s3:{}'.format(name), _S3_NS).text
AttributeError: 'NoneType' object has no attribute 'text'

During handling of the above exception, another exception occurred:

Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "minio-test.py", line 37, in <module>
    list_files("models/")
  File "minio-test.py", line 33, in list_files
    return Remote().list_files(file_dir)
  File "minio-test.py", line 25, in list_files
    for current_object in objects:
  File "/home/zupan/bin/miniconda/lib/python3.6/site-packages/minio/api.py", line 857, in list_objects
    bucket_name=bucket_name)
  File "/home/zupan/bin/miniconda/lib/python3.6/site-packages/minio/parsers.py", line 234, in parse_list_objects
    is_truncated = root.get_child_text('IsTruncated').lower() == 'true'
  File "/home/zupan/bin/miniconda/lib/python3.6/site-packages/minio/parsers.py", line 112, in get_child_text
    'Message: {}').format(self.root_name, name, error.message)
AttributeError: 'AttributeError' object has no attribute 'message'
```

minio-py version is 3.0.3


After debugging the minio-py source code, I traced the root of the problem to the difference in the returned *ListBucketResult* XML element. I found out that only for the `list_objects` and `list_objects_v2` methods GCS backend returns `<ListBucketResult xmlns=\'http://doc.s3.amazonaws.com/2006-03-01\'>` whereas all other methods that I've tested, return `<ListBucketResult xmlns="http://s3.amazonaws.com/doc/2006-03-01/">` as expected.

This is the line where it fails due to the expected _S3_NS being different than the returned URL by the GCS. https://github.com/minio/minio-py/blob/master/minio/parsers.py#L104

issue_body:

`data_sha256_hex = encode_to_hex(get_sha256(current_data)).decode('ascii')`

This is a bit of a high level overview of what we need to make nteract available as a web application in addition to components and modules for people to build their own (see #301) or for using our transforms in a standard way in JupyterLab (as @gnestor has done by grabbing the code and maintaining separately).

We ended up bringing in `react-jupyter-display-area` as well as `react-transformime` into this repository which helped us iterate faster and create a tighter build. We swore that we would export these again as part of a monorepo and that time is certainly upon us.

Here's how I imagine this repository will become laid out, as a top level:

```
packages/ # lerna style modules
electronApp/ # uses the lerna modules to compose the electron app
webApp/ # uses the lerna modules to compose a web app
```

My assumption is that the web app will build upon the jupyter notebook server. I'm (personally) not trying to support a standalone single user server for nteract, the primary reason I think I have to build a standalone frontend is to make sure that the exported components are useful while working on [a multi user server](https://github.com/nteract/commuter).

## Precursors

In order to ease maintenance burden of these modules, we've been decoupling components and reducers while also stabilizing our base. In progress issues:

* [x] [Refactor components to remove Redux logic](https://github.com/nteract/nteract/issues/1288)
* [x] Finish flow typing reducers
* [x] Refactor Rx usage to use operator patching rather than bulk import ~~`const Rx = require('rxjs/Rx')`~~

## Web app necessities

* [ ] Menu components
  * [x] Create mocks in https://github.com/nteract/mocks
  * [x] Develop components with callbacks (that someone can hook up to dispatch events for the notebook)
* [x] Dashboard - in progress in [commuter](https://github.com/nteract/commuter)

## Monorepo

This may need to be done in stages to minimize pain. Suggested pathway:

* Export everything as a mega lerna package
* Extract smaller and smaller pieces that are decoupled
  * [x] commutable
  * [x] transforms
  * [ ] display area

--------------------------------------

I'll continue to update the above with an outline as we push towards this. What else is a necessity here?

### Description

First of all thanks for the awesome work you are doing! 👍 

I am using CliMT as a wrapper for RRTMG to calculate radiative fluxes and heatingrates. For my calculations I need to change the `stellar_irradiance` to different values (working together with Sally, #19 ). While doing so I found a disagreement between the `stellar_irradiance` and the downwelling shortwave flux at the top of the atmosphere. For a zenith angle of 0 degree both values should be exactly the same. 

### What I Did

When using the CliMT default state the `stellar_irradiance` is set to `1367.0` but the downwelling shortwave flux calculated is `1414.91` which seems odd. I found that the factor of `1.035` between both values is consistent when using other `stellar_irradiance` values, too. This may point to an issue with the normalisation of the irradiance?

Unfortunately our calls to CliMT are deeply embedded in our model setup which makes it hard to copy and paste the affected code. My test simulation basically consists of a standard atmosphere without any aerosols or CFCs.

Lukas

issue_title: Automatic file transfer to allow distribution of jobs under non-shared-storage environment
issue_body:
Not sure if this is really helpful but let us say if you have access to a remote server, or virtual machine, or docker with open port, that can provide required CPU, RAM, and storage, but the remote server does not share your local storage, it would be helpful for the worker to automatically download input files and upload output files so that we can seamlessly run jobs across network. 

This is impractical for large files and small jobs, and can be useful if input and output files are not large. Implement might not be too difficult: all we need to do is to tell the remote work (when we start it), how to transfer files if the files are not available.
Cheat sheets are popular these days for quick reference to programming languages. We should have a cheat sheet for SoS that people may print / paste to their cubicle at work.

issue_title: host files for each task file.
issue_body:
The task engine of sos knows how to handle each file. For example, a remote execution host could

1. copy the local task file to remote host
2. use ssh to execute the task
3. use ssh to monitor the status of task, the `sos status task_id` command is executed on the remote host.
4. copy result file back from remote host once the task is completed.

The problem is that the task appears to be dead from a local `sos status` command. It would be useful to save host information for each task so that sos knows how to check status. For example, if

1. sos saves a `.host` file for all remote tasks.
2. local `sos status` command checks the `.host` file and ssh to remote host if necessary to get the status of the job.
4. Similarly, `sos execute` and `sos kill` should be able to start and kill a remote task from local machine.

A related question is whether or not we should leave the 'translation' step to the `execute` command. I mean, sos could

1. save a task file that is local.
2. when the task is executed by a remote host engine, the `sos execute` command performs task translation and execute it accordingly.

The advantage of this approach is that,

1. The task can be executed locally and remotely
2. we can use `sos status task` to check local status, and `sos status task -q server` to check the status of the remote file.

We could do the conversion on the fly, or use separate files for each host, or combine everything in the `task` file.

 docker run --security-opt label:disable -t -P \
    -w $PWD -v $HOME:$HOME -v /tmp:/tmp -v $PWD:$PWD -u $UID 
    -e HOME=$HOME

issue_title: stream.read(8192) on image heavy repository returns 0 despite having more data
issue_body:
I'm using gitdb in combination with GitPython to handle files directly from git repositories. So far this worked perfectly. Thanks!

Something odd is happening in a repository I'm handling at the moment. I've traced it down to the 
`read` function within the `DecompressMemMapReader`. More precisely this line of code: https://github.com/gitpython-developers/gitdb/blob/90c4f25493b918ff9dc4ee52ae8216a554bb3446/gitdb/stream.py#L275

If I understand things correctly, this check tries to enlarge the input buffer (containing compressed data) to at least 8 bytes, so the following `self._zip.decompress` call returns at last some data.

In my repository this doesn't help: `len(dcompdat)` is `0` and all the way back in my code, a `read(8192)` returns `''` despite more data being available. Changing `8192` to `8191` (or other random values) most of the time "fixes" this. I suspect this is the result of different internal buffering.

Not sure if it helps with finding a reason why `decompress` doesn't return anything from 8 input bytes, but the file responsible is an already compressed JPEG file.

How to fix this? Changing the minimum window size to 48 seems to help
```
if self._cwe - self._cws < 48:
    self._cwe = self._cws + 48
```
but I'm not sure if this has consequences I don't fully understand. Any help would be appreciated.

requirements.txt references itself: https://github.com/gitpython-developers/gitdb/blob/master/requirements.txt#L1

Just noticed it when building a recipe for conda. Happy for this to be closed immediately.

issue_title: Routing should ignore channels that have been tried
issue_body:
Thanks to the work made in [this](https://github.com/raiden-network/raiden/pull/1653) PR where the following scenario is tested:

A -> B -> C-> D

when A tries to send a payment to D and C->D has been depleted then a refund transfer is sent back and when B receives this refund transfer it queries the routing module for valid available routes and the "A->B" hop is returned. This is a bug.

Routing should ignore channels that have already been tried.

issue_title: Raiden fails on open channel with unregistered token address
issue_body:
## Problem Definition

Trying to open a channel with an unregisted token (a valid token/contract address, but which isn't yet returned in the list from `/api/1/tokens` endpoint) causes an exception in Raiden, and API request to return code 500.

### Relevant part of the exception:
```ERROR in app: Exception on /api/1/channels [PUT]
Traceback (most recent call last):
  ...
  File "/apps/raiden/raiden/api/v1/resources.py", line 55, in put
    return self.rest_api.open(**kwargs)
  File "/apps/raiden/raiden/api/rest.py", line 183, in open
    settle_timeout
  File "/apps/raiden/raiden/api/python.py", line 140, in open
    channel_manager = self.raiden.chain.manager_by_token(token_address)
  File "/apps/raiden/raiden/network/rpc/client.py", line 360, in manager_by_token
    poll_timeout=self.poll_timeout,
  File "/apps/raiden/raiden/network/rpc/client.py", line 709, in __init__
    'latest',
  File "/usr/local/lib/python2.7/dist-packages/pyethapp/rpc_client.py", line 413, in call
    raise JSONRPCClientReplyError(jsonrpc_reply.error)
JSONRPCClientReplyError: Invalid params: invalid length 0, expected a 0x-prefixed, padded, hex-encoded hash with length 40.
```

### Example requisition causing this error
```
curl -v -H "Content-Type: application/json" -X PUT -d '{"partner_address":"0x000D91Cf263a11F9BfCeE3752E5B03FC1196CE98","token_address":"0x9aBa529db3FF2D8409A1da4C9eB148879b046700","balance":0,"settle_timeout":600}' http://localhost:5001/api/1/channels
```

## Solution

Check why `manager_by_token` is failing to generate a valid `manager_address` when fed a `token_address` not yet on registry, and fix it, so it gets properly returned.


## Tasklist

- [x] Create test for opening channel with unregistered token
- [x] Fix bug so raiden acts accordingly
- [ ] Add a changelog entry

## System Description

Raiden in current master (as of 2017-07-09), running on a Linux system with Linux kernel 4.11.7, over docker. Using parity nightly, but further testing have shown it is not a parity bug, as the requisition goes empty and fails even with geth 1.6.6.

issue_title: InsufficientFunds during channel deposit causes 500 in Rest-API
issue_body:
## Problem Definition

When trying to refill a channel while not holding enough tokens to do so a `InsufficientFunds` exception is raised form the Python API which isn't caught in the Rest-API leading to `500 Internal Server Error`

## Solution

Handle `InsufficientFunds` exceptions in the Rest layer and return an appropriate response.

## Tasklist

- [x] Handle `InsufficientFunds` exceptions in the Rest layer and return an appropriate response.

issue_title: Adjust the codebase for the channel identifier change to uint256
issue_body:
## Problem Definition

After all the changes proposed by https://github.com/raiden-network/raiden-contracts/issues/193
we will need to adjust the codebase to use the new `uint256` `channel_identifier`.


## Task

Adjust the codebase for the new `channel_identifier`.
This needs some thinking so the following list may be non-exchaustive:

1. [ ] Change the type of the channel_identifier in the [typing](https://github.com/raiden-network/raiden/blob/master/raiden/utils/typing.py#L26) module.
2. [ ] Grep for the above type and follow the codepaths to make sure the appropriate type is used
3. [ ] Adjust the channel_identifier in [messages.py.](https://github.com/raiden-network/raiden/blob/4e0bfe55daea6f65ce439bc7a359b629dc0ae7cb/raiden/messages.py#L76) and make sure that a uint256 is used for signing
4. [ ] Adjust the tests where needed

Show the history of a query in a tab.

Would love to somehow do this for playground sessions as well, but tricky if two people are using the playground at the same time. Or maybe I can just segment by run_by user and it's fine.

issue_title: Allow channel name to be part of the package name spec
issue_body:
For example, to install the `gulp` package from the `javascript` channel on binstar, you would specify the dependency like this:

```
javascript/gulp
# or
https://conda.binstar.org/javascript/gulp
```

If you required a channel other than `main` (the default channel for binstar), you could specify it like this:

```
https://conda.binstar.org/javascript/channel/dev/gulp
```

Would it be possible to add a blacklist to conda in which individual packages can be excluded from the resolver? 

Sometimes a channel provides a broken or misconfigured version of package. It would be very helpful to have a simple way to tell the resolver to ignore it.

E.g. A blacklisting section in `.condarc` would do:
```
blacklist:
  - channel/my-pkg
  - channel/my-pkg-1.0.0
  - channel/my-pkg-1.*

I have broken my conda installation. I removed one package and conda delete also python interpreter

```
> conda uninstall deeptools 
Fetching package metadata ............. Solving package specifications: Package plan for package removal in environment /home/name/bin/anaconda3:

The following packages will be REMOVED:

    deeptools: 2.5.0-py35_0 bioconda
    python:    3.5.2-0              

Proceed ([y]/n)? y
So now :

>conda install python
-bash: /home/name/: /home/name/bin/anaconda3/bin/python: bad interpreter: No such file or directory

```

issue_title: Need option to disable privileged operations when creating new envs on Windows
issue_body:
Some Windows users do not have admin privileges on their boxes but they need to create conda envs.  They need a way to disable all admin operations during conda env creation and/or package install so they are not prompted for credentials.

For example, while creating an env that only contains ipython I was prompted for admin credentials on my JPM Windows laptop but without admin privileges I was had to abort the creation.  The same issue occurs when install ipython into an existing env.

conda create -n myenv ipython

issue_title: env must be deactivated/activated after installing ipython notebook
issue_body:
I've noticed that if I `conda install ipython-notebook` from within a conda environment, I have to deactivate then reactive the environment before it comes up as the latest Jupyter notebook.

Steps to reproduce:

1.

```
conda create -n test python=2
source activate test
ipython notebook
```

Launches old version of ipython notebook as expected.

2.
```
conda install ipython-notebook
ipython notebook
```

Still old version - not expected.

3.
```
source deactivate
source activate test
ipython notebook
```

Launches latest Jupyter notebook.

issue_title: Conda doesn't show progress bar and text (copy)
issue_body:
Hello!

I have conda 4.3.15 on Windows 8.1x64 (Russian interface). I have installed miniconda recently, then have added conda-canary channel and have updated conda.

When I create new environment with conda, for example:

```
conda create -p E:\Jupyter\dist\pyenv3.6-win64 --copy python=3 numpy scipy matplotlib jupyter
```

I don't see text `(copy)` in list of packages that will be installed. So I'm not sure if I will get copy of packages unstead of shortcuts.  Also I see only progressbar of packages downloading. But when packages are installing I see nothing.

Is there any fix or workarrond?

Best regards, Andrey.

issue_title: Package cache stomped over when using CONDA_SUBDIR
issue_body:
Based on the comments by Kale here:

https://github.com/conda/conda/issues/2014#issuecomment-300604221

You should be able to use the CONDA_SUBDIR environment variable to create 32-bit conda environments inside a 64-bit install. This seems to work, but has the problem that it pollutes the package cache with 32-bit packages! I tried alternating between creating 32-bit and 64-bit environments, and conda seems to be smart enough to avoid using the incorrect packages from the package cache, but since the package names don't specify bitness they get overwritten and conda has to redownload packages rather than pulling them from the cache.

CONDA_FORCE_32BIT seems to handle this better, by creating a "pkgs32" folder to cache the 32-bit packages separately. Although it sounds like that might be deprecated/removed soon. I could set CONDA_SUBDIR *and* CONDA_PKGS_DIRS when creating 32-bit environments, and unset them when creating 64-bit environments, but that seems prone to error. Are there plans to support subdir as more of a first class citizen for building packages and creating environments - e.g. allowing you to specify via the command line or in the meta.yaml/env.yaml, and keep the environments and packages separated by subdir? Or I am I better off using separate 32-bit and 64-bit coda installs?

I'm submitting a bug report for the Anaconda Windows installer, I don't know if this is the right place.  But first I'd like to thank the team for providing such great windows support for Anaconda.  Awesome job.

The bug I think is that the uninstaller tries to print too much stuff to the screen, causing it to be very slow at deleting files.  So this UI takes for ever:

![image](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/18707114/35555347-b5be5e04-0553-11e8-9546-9cf2c7ac637e.png)

## Current Behavior

The files are flashing by quickly but there are so many of them that the UI DrawText routine is taking all the time.  If one tries "rd /s /q" the folder can be deleted much more quickly than the Installer leads one to believe...

## Steps to Reproduce
Create 5 environments, each with lots of packages (100 or so), then uninstall Anaconda (conda install Jupyter should do it :-))

## Expected Behavior
The uninstaller UI could "self-throttle" the output by drawing on a separate rending thread at 30 fps whatever file name is currently being deleted, this will decouple the UI performance from the File system delete operation, and make the delete much faster.

issue_title: conda.gateways.disk.delete.rm and conda.gateways.disk.delete.retry disagree on arguments
issue_body:
Using Conda 4.5.4 with Python 2.7.14 on Windows, I'm getting the following error when using `conda remove`:

```    Traceback (most recent call last):
      File "C:\bin\Miniconda2\lib\site-packages\conda\exceptions.py", line 819, in __call__
        return func(*args, **kwargs)
      File "C:\bin\Miniconda2\lib\site-packages\conda\cli\main.py", line 78, in _main
        exit_code = do_call(args, p)
      File "C:\bin\Miniconda2\lib\site-packages\conda\cli\conda_argparse.py", line 77, in do_call
        exit_code = getattr(module, func_name)(args, parser)
      File "C:\bin\Miniconda2\lib\site-packages\conda\cli\main_install.py", line 12, in execute
        delete_trash()
      File "C:\bin\Miniconda2\lib\site-packages\conda\gateways\disk\delete.py", line 68, in delete_trash
        backoff_rmdir(path, max_tries=1)
      File "C:\bin\Miniconda2\lib\site-packages\conda\gateways\disk\delete.py", line 145, in backoff_rmdir
        _rmdir(join(root, dir))
      File "C:\bin\Miniconda2\lib\site-packages\conda\gateways\disk\delete.py", line 134, in _rmdir
        exp_backoff_fn(rmtree, path, onerror=retry, max_tries=max_tries)
      File "C:\bin\Miniconda2\lib\site-packages\conda\gateways\disk\__init__.py", line 30, in exp_backoff_fn
        result = fn(*args, **kwargs)
      File "C:\bin\Miniconda2\lib\site-packages\conda\gateways\disk\delete.py", line 212, in rmtree
        visit_files(root, files)
      File "C:\bin\Miniconda2\lib\site-packages\conda\gateways\disk\delete.py", line 200, in visit_files
        rm(unlink, join(root, target))
      File "C:\bin\Miniconda2\lib\site-packages\conda\gateways\disk\delete.py", line 196, in rm
        onerror(fn, childpath, sys.exc_info()[0])
      File "C:\bin\Miniconda2\lib\site-packages\conda\gateways\disk\delete.py", line 126, in retry
        if getattr(exc_info[1], 'errno', None) == ENOENT:
    TypeError: 'type' object has no attribute '__getitem__'
```

Looking at the Conda source, this appears to be because `conda.gateways.disk.delete.retry` expects to receive the tuple returned by of `sys.exc_info()`, so that it can analyse the exception error message, but `conda.gateways.disk.delete.rm` passes it only the first entry in that tuple (the exception type).

(There's an underlying problem that it seems the .trash directory isn't being deleted, but that should throw a more helpful error in `delete_trash`)

issue_title: conda only updates itself in the root environment
issue_body:
I was listening to a presentation about conda a couple of days ago where it was mentioned that conda aggressively auto-updates itself. Aggressive auto-updates can be problematic, because they can result in the quick spread of a buggy release that is no longer able to update itself, which has happened to me and co-workers in the past.  But in general they are a good thing, because they can help make sure that the installed user base is (a) not using outdated versions where problems were fixed long ago, and (b) are using a similar set of versions so that we don't have to reason about the effects of all possible version combinations.  

However, the main benefits of aggressively auto-updating only accrue if all or nearly all installations auto-update, which does not appear to be the case.  Right now, it looks to me like auto-updates happen only when people regularly use the root environment.  In my own workflows, I haven't installed anything in the root environment in many months; I always work on one of a number of more specialized environments instead.  I didn't even realize this until recently, when I ran into a bug fixed several months ago in conda, and which I expected not to apply to me.  I eventually fixed it by switching to the root environment and running ``conda update conda``, which was fine, but it was not at all clear that this would be needed.  I imagine other users who don't use the root environment may encounter similar issues.  I don't think conda is installed in any environment other than root, but maybe the other environments could warn on a ``conda install`` command if conda itself is more than a month or so out of date?

issue_title: Plus signs in signatures are replaced by spaces
issue_body:
Some signatures generated through lti aren't accepted by it because they contain spaces, while the signature generated to confirm the body has plus signs at those locations. I assume this occurs because `urllib.parse.parse_qs` is used in the `lti.utils` module.

For instance, calling `generate_launch_data()` on a `ToolConsumer` instance might give me `fJhMAcT9cEl4uyt3 tM q6QKPcw=` as OAuth signature, but oauthlib generates `fJhMAcT9cEl4uyt3+tM+q6QKPcw=` with the same body and secret.

issue_title: Empty error list returned when there are none
issue_body:
The [GraphQL spec](http://facebook.github.io/graphql/#sec-Errors) specifies that the errors entry in the result be non-empty if errors occurred, and should be absent entirely if no errors occurred. The graphql-core implementation returns an empty list if no errors occurred. This is an issue for compatibility with some GraphQL client libraries.

In my particular case, I have a GraphQL server using graphql-core, and a client using the Apollo JS library. There are times that Apollo thinks an error occurred when it did not, there was just an empty list of errors, when it expected an absent errors entry, null or similar. While this could certainly be fixed in Apollo, it wouldn't be a problem if graphql-core implemented the spec correctly.

I think graphql-core should return None instead of an empty list. While it's still not 100% true to the spec, it's certainly closer and would help solve issues like the above.

I have a patch which fixes this and I'd be happy to submit it if this is agreed to be a problem.

--------------
**Minimal case:**
Using graphql-core 1.0.1
```python
from graphql import (
    graphql,
    GraphQLSchema,
    GraphQLObjectType,
    GraphQLField,
    GraphQLString
)

schema = GraphQLSchema(
  query= GraphQLObjectType(
    name='RootQueryType',
    fields={
      'hello': GraphQLField(
        type= GraphQLString,
        resolver=lambda *_: 'world'
      )
    }
  )
)

query = '{ hello }'

result = graphql(schema, query)

print(result.errors)

```
Prints `[]`
Expected `None`

I'm running into a bug using the `async`/`await` keywords and the AsyncioExecutor. Here is my test:

```python

from graphql.execution.executors.asyncio import AsyncioExecutor
import graphene

class Query(graphene.ObjectType):

    recipes = graphene.String()

    async def resolve_recipes(self, *_):
        return 'hello'


schema = graphene.Schema(
    query=Query,
    executor=AsyncioExecutor()
)

executed = schema.execute("""
    query {
        recipes
    }
""")

print(executed.data)
print(executed.errors)
```

which results in the following messages logged in my terminal:
```
/Users/alec/bin/python/graphql/execution/executor.py:110: RuntimeWarning: coroutine 'Query.resolve_recipes' was never awaited
  result = resolve_field(exe_context, parent_type, source_value, field_asts)
OrderedDict([('recipes', '<coroutine object Query.resolve_recipes at 0x10eb0f360>')])
[]
````

this does not work,

    sqlcmd -A -U sa

this works

    sqlcmd -A

And then hitting enter at `sa`

issue_title: which is the license? all rights reserved, or BSD?
issue_body:
the LICENSE file here states "all rights reserved", but the pypi page says it's BSD.
which is the case? 
(I am choosing a library for a GPL licensed program.)

_lost_point_level1() calculates the total number of a pixel's adjacent pixels whose color is the same as that pixel, by iterate through row_range (-1, 0, 1) and col_range (-1, 0, 1).
e.g. in a 3x3 sub matrix and center pixel color is black, count the number of black pixels in 8
border pixels. And then use later for the penalty point count.
But I think That's incorrect. Rule 1 should only rule rows, or columns.
i.e. 5 consecutive color in a row: drop 3 points. 6 consecutive color in a column: drop 4 points.
Here's the rule description in *ISOIEC 18004_2006Cor 1_2009.pdf* [download link](https://qrcodenet.codeplex.com/):

|Feature|Evaluation condition|Points|
|---------|---------------|-------------------|
|Adjacent modules in row/column in same color|No. of modules = (5 + i)|N 1 + i|
|Block of modules in same color|Block size = m × n|N 2 × (m - 1) × (n - 1)|

Rule 2 is for block. So rule 1 won't rule block. Rule 1 should just rule a row, or a column.
Below all agree with the second interpretation and implementation:
1. `IDFS_2012_QR code_suplement.pdf` download link same as above.
2. [Thonky's tutorial](http://www.thonky.com/qr-code-tutorial/data-masking) site contains the same picture as 1's pdf, maybe from the same author?
3. [Zebra crossing](https://github.com/zxing/zxing/blob/master/core/src/main/java/com/google/zxing/qrcode/encoder/MaskUtil.java#L37-L42): Popular Java qrcode generator that has 10,000+ stars.

Maybe I'll file a pull request for this if y'all don't mind and if my implementation is correct and pass the correctness test.
By the way, is the algorithm lower bound O(n\*n) for iterating through a 2d matrix?
Is there any algorithm better than O(n\*n) for this penalty rule 1?
Best regards.

issue_title: Invalid CSS after "": expected media query list, was "992px") when using media queries
If-Match: * does not match the spec
issue_body: Per https://github.com/Kinto/kinto/pull/1064#issuecomment-279351031, the behavior of `If-Match: *` is the opposite of `If-None-Match: *`. `If-None-Match: *` should succeed if no resource exists at the given URL and fail if any resource exists at the given URL, but `If-Match: *` should fail if no resource exists at the given URL and succeed if any resource exists at the given URL. Currently `If-Match: *` does nothing (it always succeeds).

issue_title: [V3] Make test fixtures accessible in pip-installed package
issue_body:
### Type:

- [X] Suggestion
- [ ] Bug

### Brief description of the problem
There are some nice test fixtures in `tests/conftest.py`, but no good way to import them for use with third party cogs. These tools should made available in the pip deployment for conscientious cog developers.

As an example, I tried copying that file to `site-packages/redbot/testing/conftest.py` and then having `from redbot.testing.conftest import *` in my cog repo's test file. The fixtures were then available as expected. Such a solution might be good enough, but it'd be better to find a way that doesn't involve `import *`. Perhaps it's unavoidable, with the way `pytest` wants to do namespace abuse for fixtures.

issue_title: how can I search for the specific strings or specific APIs if they have been used within the app or not?
issue_body:
Hello 

I know that if want to search for classes or methods and I can use the Analysis object (the dx from AnalyzeAPK.However, when I print "dx",  it told me there are more than 10000 classes. my app has just 10 classes. how can I show just my classes within the app? 
I used this command: 
```
 a,d,dx = AnalyzeAPK("/test.apk")
```
when I print just dx
```
In [70]: dx
Out[70]: <analysis.Analysis VMs: 2, Classes: 10376, Strings: 19630>
```

I have another question: 
how can I search for the specific strings or specific APIs if they have been used within the app or not?

The `gwcs.WCS.__call__` function has a `with_units` parameter that can be used to return an `astropy.coordinates.SkyCoord` object with units.

But if one tries to round trip this by taking this `astropy.coordinates.SkyCoord` object and feed it back into the `backward_transform`, then it fails, as it's expecting N inputs, where N is the number of dimensions inside the `SkyCoord` object.

Following the example from the docs, we construct a simplified `gwcs.WCS` object:
```
import numpy as np
from astropy.modeling.models import (Shift, Scale, Rotation2D,
      Pix2Sky_TAN, RotateNative2Celestial)
det2sky = (Shift(-10.5) & Shift(-13.2) | Rotation2D(0.0023) |
           Scale(.01) & Scale(.04) | Pix2Sky_TAN() |
           RotateNative2Celestial(5.6, -72.05, 180)).rename("det2sky")

from astropy import units as u
from astropy import coordinates as coord
from gwcs import coordinate_frames as cf
detector_frame = cf.Frame2D(name="detector", unit=(u.pix, u.pix))
sky_frame = cf.CelestialFrame(reference_frame=coord.ICRS(), name='icrs')

from gwcs import wcs
pipeline = [(detector_frame, det2sky),
            (sky_frame, None)
            ]
wcsobj = wcs.WCS(pipeline)
```
And then test it out:

issue_title: Subscribe to subprocess not receiving with multiple nnpy channels
issue_body:
Problem
-----------
When I have two `nnpy.Socket`s, a `REQ` socket in the main thread, and a `SUB` socket created later in a `multiprocessing.Process`, the `SUB` socket never receives anything. This problem does not occur for:

- Single `SUB` socket in a `multiprocessing.Process`.
- `SUB` and `REQ` sockets both in the main thread.
- `SUB` socket in a `threading.Thread` and `REQ` socket in the main thread (but I have no choice but to use `multiprocessing.Process` for other reasons)

Minimal Example
---------------------

**main.py**
```py
import nnpy
import multiprocessing

cmd_socket = nnpy.Socket(nnpy.AF_SP, nnpy.REQ)
cmd_socket.bind("ipc:///tmp/nnpy-test-cmd")

cmd_socket.send("start")
assert cmd_socket.recv() == "Starting!"

def run_sub():
    sub_socket = nnpy.Socket(nnpy.AF_SP, nnpy.SUB)
    sub_socket.setsockopt(nnpy.SUB, nnpy.SUB_SUBSCRIBE, "topic:")
    sub_socket.connect("ipc:///tmp/nnpy-test-pub")

    while True:
        print "received " + repr(sub_socket.recv())

sub = multiprocessing.Process(target=run_sub)
sub.daemon = True
sub.start()
sub.join()
```

**child.py**
```py
import nnpy
import time

cmd_socket = nnpy.Socket(nnpy.AF_SP, nnpy.REP)
cmd_socket.connect("ipc:///tmp/nnpy-test-cmd")

assert 'start' == cmd_socket.recv()

pub_socket = nnpy.Socket(nnpy.AF_SP, nnpy.PUB)
pub_socket.bind("ipc:///tmp/nnpy-test-pub")

cmd_socket.send("Starting!")

for n in range(10):
    pub_socket.send("topic:It is now " + str(time.time()) + " o'clock!")
    time.sleep(0.25)
```

This code works if we either remove the `'start'` `'Starting!'` request/response, *or* run `run_sub` in the main thread or a `threading.Thread` instead of in a `multiprocessing.Process`.

[Here are test cases with more print statements that show the cases where this works and does not work](https://github.com/nanomsg/nnpy/files/342197/test-cases.zip).

The problem is definitely on the receiving end, as the `SUB`/`PUB` socket can be subscribed to and receives the messages in other programs.

For Scala and R support, we may want to look at http://pygments.org/languages/

For SQL support, look at @alope107's solution in https://github.com/alope107/spark-sql-magic/commit/34b4d864cdaaa0cc56481b3045a66bf679141f78
Consolidate magics and commands.  Clean up UX.

issue_title: Improperly labeled sbo values for biomass growth objective function
issue_body:
#### Problem description

While looking at the various issues revolving around the `find_biomass_precursors` function, Christian and I discovered that the sbo term used to search for and annotate biomass production was improperly labeled. 

It was `SBO: 0000630`, which is actually the sbo code for ATP maintenance, instead of its actual label, `SBO: 0000629`. 

This caused a bug in which the ATPM reaction in models was tested on for biomass growth instead of the actual biomass objective functions. It was also an issue in the `test_for_helpers.py` class, but we believe this issue probably exists throughout the memote code. We will eventually need to go through the code and ensure this problem does not exist elsewhere, and if it does, we will need to fix it.

issue_title: Commissioner/Joiner Router port usage is incorrect in Joiner Authentication
issue_body:
According to the the 1.1 spec, during the Joiner Authentication process, the Joiner Router should send to the Border Agent from an ephemeral port to port MM (61631), and should receive replies from the Border Agent on this ephemeral port.

joiner_router.cpp is not listening on this ephemeral port after sending the RLY_RX. Further, the Commissioner is then processing the DTLS-ClientHello and sending the RLY_TX to a hard-coded port (MM, 61631), where the Joiner Router is listening (which keeps things working in OpenThread, but is incompatible with the spec).

So there are two parts to this issue:
1) The joiner router should be listening/expecting the RLY_TX reply on whatever port it sends from rather than only on 61631. My (and I assume other 3rd party) off mesh border agent/commissioner which don't send to port 61631 end up going through the generic HandleUdpReceive path which breaks the joiner authentication.
2) The commissioner/border agent (these two roles seem to be treated the same in the codebase) should not be sending to a hard coded port 61631. A joiner router that is waiting for the RLY_TX on the port it sent from would not expect this port to be used for the reply, so OpenThread would be incompatible with third party Joiner Routers.

When the time is right, we should cut an initial [release](https://help.github.com/articles/creating-releases/) (maybe after I/O?) Items I think we could include in the release description can include:
- Supported Thread spec version
- Spec features added, enhanced or changed
- Device, library or framework support
- Docs and tools improvements

Anything else?

issue_title: NCP-UART: assert when used with CDC
issue_body:
One of my devices uses USB CDC protocol as a UART channel. This means that before the VCOM is opened on the host, no data can be sent and as such otPlatUartSend returns an error, which causes an assert in `ncp_uart.cpp`, when sending an initial status update (even before wpantund sends any information).

I've changed otPlatUartSend code to always return no error and this seems to work, however I have few questions.
1) Are there any potentials problems (NCP-wise) with otPlatUartSend always returning success, even though if the actual data is not sent (at least initially)?
2) What's the purpose of sending the initial status update before any messages are received from the host? In real UART case there is no way of knowing that the host is listening.

Also this does not look like such a critical area to cause an assert. Here's few ideas how it could be changed:
1) Ignore the returned status of otPlatUartSend.
2) Add flag which enables sending messages only after information is received from host.
3) Add a forth `UartTxState` state `kSendingFrame`, which would retry the otPlatUartSend until success.

Any thoughts?

issue_title: Chromedriver does not work under windows (includes fix)
issue_body:
WebDriverException 

"ChromeDriver executable needs to be available in the path. \
Please download from http://code.google.com/p/selenium/downloads/list\
and read up at http://code.google.com/p/selenium/wiki/ChromeDriver")

This happens even when chromedriver is in path. This occurs because selenium is calling subprocess.Popen which splinter monkeypatches (bad idea imo).

This is done in splinter\driver\webdriver\__init__.py line ~39

        # selenium is such a verbose guy let's make it open the
        # browser without showing all the meaningless output
        def MyPopen(*args, **kw):
            kw['stdout'] = TemporaryFile()
            kw['stderr'] = TemporaryFile()
            kw['close_fds'] = True
            return self.old_popen(*args, **kw)

And if you read from http://docs.python.org/library/subprocess.html

"Note that on Windows, you cannot set close_fds to true and also redirect the standard handles by setting stdin, stdout or stderr."

Issue can be resolved by commenting out "kw['close_fds'] = True"

issue_title: Log output for error response summary is incomplete
issue_body:
When an API error is returned, the SOAP response is expected to be logged. Currently, it literally prints the suds.sax.document.Document instance containing that response, rather than the human-readable string.

The fix for this is pretty simple, we'll need to modify the following line in **common.py**:

```
        _logger.info('SOAP response:\n%s', e.document)
```

To:

```
        _logger.info('SOAP response:\n%s', e.document.str())
```

We'll push this fix out in the next release.

issue_title: What are the expected performance characteristics on linux-arm
issue_body:
I've tried the 2.1 preview1 build on my ASP.NET Core application. Most of my code is in `netstandard2.0` libraries and I have added `netcoreapp2.1` to my target frameworks in addition to `net47`. The result just works - wonderful!

The application runs on my embedded Linux platform based on Yocto 2.3 (poky) with an i.MX 6 SoloX. I have published it as `linux-arm`, deployed it and can successfully run it with `DOTNET_SYSTEM_GLOBALIZATION_INVARIANT=1` (because I'm lazy) - yay!

I can, however, compare the performance of the .NET Core build to the `net47` build running via Mono 5.4.1.6. .NET Core 2.1 takes about double the time compared to Mono to fully start the application and all first web requests (Web API calls or SignalR) feel a lot slower on .NET Core as well. Subsequent calls, though, are fast.

So my question is: What are the expected performance characteristics of the preview1 build and what can I expect towards 2.1 RTM for `linux-arm`?

issue_title: Side panel: Worksheet permissions not displaying correctly
issue_body:
Repro:

- Go to any dashboard
- Click on one of the worksheets under "My Worksheets" -- not on the name, one the white space so the side panel updates
- Notice that the permissions are all empty

Problem: When a worksheet loads, all of the information needed to render the worksheet is interpreted by the server and then loaded to the client (it's a big data dump). The "My Worksheets" table is built using a `wsearch` in Markdown, and so when the table is generated only the information needed to display the table is sent to the client. The permission information of the worksheet is not sent. When the user clicks the table row of the worksheet and it is displayed in the side panel, there are no API calls involved -- the client simply displays the cached data about the worksheet, even though it does not include some of the needed information.

Proposed solutions:

- Call the REST API every time a new worksheet is selected and the side panel needs to update
- Change the interpretation code so that all of the data needed to display permissions is loaded to the client

I'm using octave_kernel, but I'm now certain there is no (easy) way to fix my problem without changing process_metakernel in Calysto. When I run a command that takes a long time to return a prompt, or any kind of text for that matter, I get the dreaded pexpect 30-second default timeout. 

A "fix" for this was committed almost a year ago (8b4429ffafbc201b4e8af5bcbe00ac647a8176f0). However, the critical part (`timeout=None`) was reverted barely a week later (`timeout=-1`, d9c975538b30061a7a6a62bedc4e8b2fc4447f88), along with changes to pexpect itself that changed the behavior of `timeout=None` to just use the default timeout of 30 seconds.

I have to admit I understand very little of this, but I suspect the author(s) of these changes consider the original bug to be only an issue when there is a continuous stream of output from a long-running process. I would ask that they consider the case when a process runs quietly for a long time. I realize this is probably an edge case, but at least for the kind of work I do with Octave, it is not all that uncommon.

Maybe there is a "fix" that at least allows the child kernel (octave_kernel in this case) to specify the timeout. As it stands, my only options are to manually modify the pexpect files, or to modify my Octave code to spit out a stream of output that prevents the timeout from triggering. Neither of these are very satisfying.

As it stands, my only options are to manually modify the pexpect files, or to modify my Octave code to spit out a stream of output that prevents the timeout from triggering. Neither of these are very satisfying.

issue_title: Use Linux distro CA certs when available
issue_body:
Most Linux distros already provide a trusted set of CA certificates with tools to adjust the certificates as needed. It would be really nice if certifi (i.e. requests) used these rather than the shipped CA bundle. As it turns out, at least Fedora, Debian and SUSE patch their certifi to use the system CA bundle:

https://src.fedoraproject.org/rpms/python-certifi/blob/master/f/certifi-2016.9.26-remove-bundle-cert.patch
https://salsa.debian.org/debian/python-certifi/blob/debian/master/debian/patches/0001-Use-Debian-provided-etc-ssl-certs-ca-certificates.cr.patch
https://build.opensuse.org/package/view_file/devel:languages:python/python-certifi/python-certifi-shipped-requests-cabundle.patch?expand=1

That's all well and good until pip pulls in a new version of certifi and now you're back to the bundled CA certificates.

Would you accept a patch that tries the above paths on Linux before falling back to the bundled CA certs?

When adding the RAVEN_CONFIG to django settings.py, I had to add a '..' to the path to get the root of the git repository.

With the current instructions it says that the settings.py folder is not the root of a git repository

```
raven.exceptions.InvalidGitRepository: /path/to/settings/ does not seem to be the root of a git repository
```

Specifically the [following line](https://github.com/getsentry/raven-python/blob/master/raven/contrib/flask.py#L152) in the extension will raise a TypeError:

`is_authenticated = current_user.is_authenticated()`

Switching to:

``is_authenticated = current_user.is_authenticated`

as well as two other `.is_anonymous()` calls, resolves the problem.

issue_title: don't just ignore oversized stack frame local in get_frame_locals
issue_body:
```
def get_frame_locals(frame, transformer=transform, max_var_size=4096):
...
    for k, v in iteritems(f_locals):
        v = transformer(v)
        v_size = len(repr(v))
        if v_size + f_size < 4096:
            f_vars[k] = v
            f_size += v_size
```

That will just leave out the oversized variable WITHOUT ANY NOTICE. Come on! If it's oversized at least add ```f_vars[k] = "<Value omitted because it's too big>"```. Otherwise I can debug all day why it's missing.

BTW, the `max_var_size` parameter seems to be a decoy here.

issue_title: Performance improvement: Use cache to store failed logins
issue_body:
Performance improvement: Use cache to store failed logins, SQL queries are slow. Django-axes should use Django's cache framework for storing failed logins.

For example, you guys could store keys with the format `axes-<hash>` where the hash is `md5(ip+ua)`. The data for that key should be the number of failed logins sent by that ip+ua and the timeout for the key is the `AXES_COOLOFF_TIME`

It would be great to have a method (for a view) and/or template tag to know if user is locked out. 

E.g. the lockout view is shown based on URL. It is preserved in user browser history. So a user can, even by mistake, get to that page again and again see the lockout message, which may not valid. It would be good to redirect the user, if they are not actually locked out.

issue_title: API Gateway CreateRestApi returns incorrect timestamp format
issue_body:
I’m using Terraform to try to create an API Gateway REST API. Under the hood Terraform uses `aws-sdk-go`, the Go version of Amazon’s SDK. When Terraform/`aws-sdk-go` calls the endpoint to create a REST API, this is the response returned from LocalStack:

```json
{"description": null, "createdDate": "2017-12-14T23:07:15.206Z", "id": "58A-Z620A-Z560", "name": "lambda-api-hello-gw"}
```

And `aws-sdk-go` crashes on this:

```
* aws_api_gateway_rest_api.main: Error creating API Gateway:
SerializationError: failed decoding JSON RPC response
caused by: unsupported value: <nil> (*time.Time)
```

The response from real AWS, which doesn’t crash `aws-sdk-go`, is this:

```json
{"createdDate":1513282360,"endpointConfiguration":{"ipv6":false,"types":["EDGE"]},"id":"rnh2djtis0","name":"lambda-api-hello-gw"}
```

Note that `createdDate` is `"2017-12-14T23:07:15.206Z"` from LocalStack and `1513282360` from AWS. I suspect this is the problem. (For some reason, both the Python and Node AWS clients are fine with LocalStack’s response; only the Go client chokes on it.)

I’m trying to find where in the code I can convert the format of this timestamp, but I’m stumped. I can’t quite figure out how `generic_proxy.py` and `apigateway_listener.py` work together; it seems that the request from Terraform is getting proxied through LocalStack to some other process, which is a running Moto server? How does this work?

Within Moto’s code, it seems pretty clear that the offending line is here:

https://github.com/spulec/moto/blob/e75f3ef4d4f7b0fa7e14dbb4431d379b68ae5502/moto/apigateway/models.py#L311-L317

How would we go about updating this? A PR to that repo, and then an update to the `requirements.txt` in LocalStack? Or does LocalStack modify the response from Moto server, fixing issues like this?

issue_title: Test examples applications in continuous integration.
issue_body: Currently, it's possible to change the codebase and break the example applications without triggering any kind of test failure. So we end up testing the example applications by hand. We should do this automatically in Jenkins.

See "Automatic memory management" at https://redis.io/topics/modules-intro.

This should help avoid memory leaks and simplify the code.

cc @pcmoritz @imzhenyu

issue_title: How to improve ray log experience
issue_body:
Recently, we are trying to improve the ray log experience to better support user debugging.

Imaging that a user write a print function in his remote functions to output some debugging information. His script fails for some reason. He wants to find the log, but he does not know which machine his task is running. There are tons of logs in a big cluster. Even though he manages to find out the running machine. It is hard to find out the log from many worker logs. 

We are trying to write programs to provide users with the target log. However, one worker will be assigned with tasks from different drivers. That is to say in one worker log file, there will be output from different task of different drivers or even different users. 

Is there a way to output the log according to driver ID or output the message with the driver ID?
We need better reporting for issues with the Pandas version on a user's system.

`ssladapter.py` makes an attempt to import `urllib3` from `requests` and if it fails, attempts to import `urllib3` directly -- see https://github.com/docker/docker-py/blob/453964466741a1c85fc420c8b40fb5710f40b017/docker/transport/ssladapter.py#L10-L13.  This may result in an older version of `urllib3` being used at runtime.  For example, the line at https://github.com/docker/docker-py/blob/453964466741a1c85fc420c8b40fb5710f40b017/docker/transport/ssladapter.py#L22 fails for versions of `urllib3` before version 1.8 with the following exception:

```
AttributeError: 'module' object has no attribute 'connection'
```

Proposal for resolution: Specify `urllib3>=1.8` in `requirements.txt` and `setup.py`, import directly from `urllib3` rather than import from the version bundled w/ `requests`.

issue_title: Following suggestions for image.save results in really slow saves
issue_body:
I'm trying to write out an image which has a modest number of unionfs layers (5) and which is pretty small when I do docker image ls (~200MiB). The code I use to save the image looks like this:

```py
    with open(outputImageFileName, 'wb', buffering=0) as outputImage:
        with deployImage.save() as imageBytes:
            for chunk in tqdm(imageBytes, leave=True, miniters=1):
                outputImage.write(chunk)
```

Saving the image this way takes well upwards of 28 minutes on a brand new MacBook pro with plenty RAM and big SSD whereas from the command line it is nearly instant. 

For what it's worth, when I tried to do a little detective work, I found that the current version of urllib3 has arguments to the call to `stream` which, amongst other things, makes [it possible to control the size of chunks being transferred](http://urllib3.readthedocs.io/en/latest/reference/#urllib3.response.HTTPResponse.stream); however, this interface does not appear to be supported on the  object that are returned by `Image.save()`

issue_title: Maryland AG opinions have wrong dates
issue_body:
I'm seeing two problems, one easy and minor, the other a real issue. 

1. The minor issue is that we are approximating dates to be July 2nd of the current year. Of course, that date hasn't happened yet this year, so we should make sure we don't do that. (A user complained about this today).

2. The bigger problem is that the dates on the backscraped content are wrong. For example: 

https://www.courtlistener.com/opinion/4344857/countiescode-home-rule-counties-power-to-regulate-the-formation-of/

That case is from 2013, but it has a date from July 2nd, 2017. I took a glance at the code for this and I didn't really follow what it was supposed to do. 

I think if we can fix the scraper code, the easiest thing will be to delete the content that we presently have and just re-scrape the website. The harder way (which would preserve the URLs we currently have) is to write a script to fix the content we have.This isn't terribly hard either, if we can create a CSV or something with the correct data. (I could probably even do this in the Python shell if I had a CSV to work with.)

issue_body: There are a number of optimizers such as LBFGS and Eve (https://arxiv.org/pdf/1611.01505.pdf) that require access to the loss function value to calculate. The current interface doesn't support this, so it should be added.

Hello, I am trying to build a simple app for Android platform. Buildozer works well with Kivy VM but not on my own system. I really want to use buildozer on my system. Here is the code and log.

**Code**: 
 ```python
import kivy
from kivy.app import App

from kivy.uix.label import Label
from kivy.uix.textinput import TextInput
from kivy.uix.gridlayout import GridLayout

class LoginScreen(GridLayout):
    def __init__(self, **kwargs):
        super(LoginScreen, self).__init__(**kwargs)
        self.cols = 2

        self.add_widget(Label(text='Username: '))
        self.username = TextInput(multiline=False)
        self.add_widget(self.username)

        self.add_widget(Label(text='Password'))
        self.password = TextInput(multiline=False, password=True)
        self.add_widget(self.password)
        
class TestApp(App):
    def build(self):
        return LoginScreen()

if __name__ == '__main__':

    app.run()

issue_title: Facing issues in making webbrowser.open(url) work
issue_body:
I am to make an android webview app with p4a using kivy example.
```import kivy                                                                                     
from kivy.app import App                                                                        
from kivy.lang import Builder                                                                   
from kivy.utils import platform                                                                 
from kivy.uix.widget import Widget  
import webbrowser                                                            
from kivy.clock import Clock                                                                    
from jnius import autoclass                                                                     
from android.runnable import run_on_ui_thread                                                   
WebView = autoclass('android.webkit.WebView')                                                   
WebViewClient = autoclass('android.webkit.WebViewClient')                                       
activity = autoclass('org.kivy.android.PythonActivity').mActivity
urlc='https://www.google.co.in'
url='https://www.google.co.in'                            
                                                                                                
class Wv(Widget):                                                                               
    def __init__(self, **kwargs):                                                               
        super(Wv, self).__init__(**kwargs)                                                      
        Clock.schedule_once(self.create_webview, 0)                                             
                                                                                                
    @run_on_ui_thread                                                                           
    def create_webview(self, *args):                                                            
        webview = WebView(activity)                                                             
        webview.getSettings().setJavaScriptEnabled(True)
        settings.setSupportZoom(True) # enables zoom
        settings.setBuiltInZoomControls(True) # enables zoom controls                                        
        wvc = WebViewClient();                                                                  
        webview.setWebViewClient(wvc);                                                          
        activity.setContentView(webview)                                                        
        # webview.loadUrl('https://www.google.co.in')
        if (url.find(urlc)==-1):
            webbrowser.open(url)
        else:
            webview.loadUrl(url)
                                                                                                
class ServiceApp(App):
	def on_pause(self):
		return True
	def on_resume(self):
		pass
    def build(self):
        return Wv()                                                                             
                                                                                                
if __name__ == '__main__':                                                                      
    ServiceApp().run()```


I am trying for a behavior that all external link should open in webbrowser and internal link in webview.
But with above code app shows all links in webview only.
The command used to make apk:
p4a apk --private /home/user/kiviapps/webview/ --package=org.example.app1 --name "appname" --version 0.1 --bootstrap=sdl2 --requirements=kivy==master,pyjnius,android --permission INTERNET

issue_title: Annoyances while writing frontend for TensorFlow
issue_body:
As reported by @fmassa:

Here are some differences I found that were a bit annoying
- need to create extra graph nodes because Reshape and Transpose requires the `shape`/`permutation` as input, and not attributes. Also, due to the default value of `Transpose` in ONNX (revert all dimensions), I need to get the information of the dimension of the node that is transposed to be able to recreate this node
- TF doesn’t accept NCHW for MaxPool2d on the CPU, need to add transposes there or always use NHWC (edited)

110 libs.append(os.path.join(os.getenv('PROTOBUF_LIBDIR'), "libprotobuf")) should be protobuf?
e.g. if we set PROTOBUF_LIBDIR to be /usr/local/lib, it would yield a warning: 
**no library file corresponding to '/usr/local/lib/libprotobuf' found (skipping)**
after change to protobuf, everything works fine.
based upon the support that we provide here in the libfv library

issue_title: Modify git commit SHA1 status label format
issue_body:
Will be revised to the following format:

```
[abcd123]
[abcd123]-dev
[abcd123]-release
```

from 

```
abcd123
abcd123-dev
abcd123-release
```


as part of the new version specification that is in development.  This facilitates parsing of the SHA1 hash string.

See: https://github.com/source-foundry/font-v/issues/19#issuecomment-347990953

issue_body:
I am using Jupyter notebooks to access the the ADGCv2 or Opendatencube, as it now is called. 
I did some band math, masking and so on with S2A-L1C data and plotted the results using matplotlib. Suddenly I got a ``MemoryError``.  When I checked my CentOS machine, all 32GB of memory where permanently allocated. When I restarted the kernel in the notebook, all the memory was freed again.

I investigated this a bit and it seems there is a quite serious memory leak happening when using ``%matplotlib inline`` in a Jupyter notebook. Every time I plot a figure with matplotlib, some amount of memory is allocated (e.g. 2GB for a more complex plot) but not freed after the plot is finished. When plotting again the some bahaviour is shown, leading in a bigger notebook to a ``MemoryError at`` some point. 

I could find a unresolved ticket regarding this problem in the [Ipython repo](https://github.com/ipython/ipython/issues/7270)

In the above ticket, it is suggest to use ``matplotlib.use('agg')`` instead of ``%matplotlib inline``. This indeed solves the memory leak, but I always need to save the figures to the disk in order to see them. It would be way nicer to see them directly in the notebook. 

I am aware that I can restart the kernel when the ``MemoryError`` occurs and then go on with my calculations, but this is not a very convenient solution either as I would need to load all the needed products again. 

Has anyone else experienced the some problem and/or found a solution?
What is the reasoning for using the class name 'Section' for nodes in the pagetree? I think it would make more sense to call them Pages.

Hey guys!

When inheriting from a task class that has a `task_namespace`, imho it would be pythonic that the inheriting class has the same `task_namespace` (if not set otherwise). However, this does not seem to be the case. Is this behavior intended by design?

### Possible solution

Due to the way the task register meta class implements the `_default_namespace` fallback, the proper namespace is only set when it's contained in the `class_dict`: ([link](https://github.com/spotify/luigi/blob/master/luigi/task_register.py#L67))

```python
class Register(abc.ABCMeta):

    def __new__(metacls, classname, bases, classdict):
        if "task_namespace" not in classdict:
            classdict["task_namespace"] = metacls._default_namespace

        cls = super(Register, metacls).__new__(metacls, classname, bases, classdict)
        metacls._reg.append(cls)
```

The fix would be quite simple:

```python
class Register(abc.ABCMeta):

    def __new__(metacls, classname, bases, classdict):
        cls = super(Register, metacls).__new__(metacls, classname, bases, classdict)

        if not getattr(cls, "task_namespace", None):
            cls.task_namespace = metacls._default_namespace

        metacls._reg.append(cls)
```

This implies a minor change though: the inherited namespace would be favored over the default one, except when the inherited one is falsy.

Any thoughts?

issue_title: 关于使用 "dns over https" 来解决dns污染以及acl过滤的设想。
issue_body:
如题，最近在研究如何给ss添加一个acl过滤的功能，最初的构思跟ss-anroid差不多，即通过ss-tunnel远程转发dns请求，然后根据返回的ip来选择是否走代理。但是这里有一个非常棘手的问题：一些国内的大型网站（比如youku、baidu）通常都会在国外设置镜像服务器或cdn，这样的话远程请求到的ip就是国外的ip了。如果用这个ip去做acl过滤的话显然不理想。

所以我就在想能不能找到更好的方式。试着goolge了一下，发现 edns协议 可以实现这个功能，它的实现原理是在原来的dns请求包中加上请求方的ip，dns服务器在收到这个请求包的时候会对这个ip做一些处理（为保证用户的隐私，仅保留ip的前三位），然后交给服务商的dns去处理，服务商的dns在接到请求后会根据此ip来返回相应的结果（一般情况下会返回距离最近的ip）。这个 edns 协议是google提出的，很多dns服务器都已经支持了该协议，谷歌的 8.8.8.8 更是早就支持了。但是我用一些支持edns的工具去测的时候，发现偶尔还是会返回国外的地址，及其不稳定。

**最终，我找到了一个非常稳定的方案，即谷歌提供的 “dns over https” 服务，顾名思义，就是通过https来查询dns，这项服务支持 edns。地址在这里 ： https://dns.google.com 。请求的时候只要带上 `EDNS Client Subnet` 参数就能远程解析到正确的国内地址。返回的数据是Json格式的，非常方便。**

比如下面这个链接：
https://dns.google.com/query?name=www.youku.com&type=A&dnssec=false&ecs=114.114.1.1

可以看到，如果不加 `EDNS Client Subnet` 参数的话，返回的就是国外的地址，如果加上的话，返回的就是国内的地址。这简直是太爽了！！！
其中的 114.114.1.1 就是请求方的ip，可以任意填，也可以带掩码，不一定要填真实ip。

**有了这个服务，dns污染和acl过滤都不再是问题！**

issue_title: dockerflow.logging.JsonLogFormatter doesn't handle extra arguments that are not json-serializable
issue_body:
Enabling `dockerflow.logging.JsonLogFormatter` in your project can cause `TypeError` exceptions when extra arguments is passed to the logging call.

```python
>>> from logging import getLogger
>>> getLogger('foo').warning('warning with extra info', extra={'something': object()})
Traceback (most recent call last):
(...)
TypeError: <object object at 0x7f5e88a54630> is not JSON serializable
```

It's a perfect valid logging call, you might have other formatters configured taking advantage of the extra data. Django does that in some of its core, for instance [passing `request` objects when logging 404 or 500 request errors](https://docs.djangoproject.com/en/1.10/topics/logging/#django-request).

issue_title: `letsencrypt renew` doesn't update outdated symlinks in live directory
issue_body:
I had a case reported here:
https://community.letsencrypt.org/t/renew-not-working-for-expired-certificate/17883

`letsencrypt renew` managed to get & write new certificates to `/etc/letsencrypt/archive/` but didn't update the symlinks in `/etc/letsencrypt/live/` (probably I didn't run the command as root -- my error).

Subsequent runs of `letsencrypt renew` told me my certificate was updated but the `live` symlink didn't change from '1' to '2' files so I thought the renewal wasn't working properly.

I think `letsencrypt renew` should try to defend against errors like this by checking the `live` symlinks point to the latest version of the `archive` files, even when it doesn't request a renewal.

issue_title: suggestion to add REMOVE DOMAIN  command, and how to implement it
issue_body:
A `certbot certificates` command produces for instance

* *"Certificate Name:"* `xxxx.org`
* *"Domain:"* `aaaaa.com  aaaaa.org  bbbbb.com`
 
The command `certbot --cert-name`  can only be the name shown on *CertificateName*... It can't be a name of the  *Domain* line, **but this is the most frequent need of users!**

To *delete an item* from the *Domains* list (eg. to delete `bbbbb.com`), user must to  run `certbot --cert-name xxxx.org -d aaaaa.com -d aaaaa.org` and omitting from the `-d` list the item to be deleted, as [remembered here](https://community.letsencrypt.org/t/remove-domain-not-required-from-cert/14010/25?u=ppkrauss)... No news about it.

So the algorithm is very simple!

* *Domain* exist and we can suppose that is an array.
* the item to be deleted is supplyed as input, and can be removed from the *Domain* array.
* the effect of `certbot --cert-name` command is well-known by user (replace the existing certificate by a new certificate containing only the domains specified by `-d` options).

... And it's very useful:

* redo a list by hand **is error-prone**
* a big list is very common, and **so boring** to redo, even by copy/paste.
* not make sense the user redo it, if the command knows it!

Hello there

I'm trying to generate QNames from URIs using the `namespace_manager.normalizeUri` function, but I ran into some weird behaviour: 

```
In [1]: uri1 = u"http://www.springernature.com/scigraph/things/contributions/b5c40759f52045a0f6e7c73befe3bd74"

In [2]: uri2 = u"http://www.springernature.com/scigraph/things/contributions/980631eef1a62ad91b437f5e50691bff"

In [3]: graph = rdflib.Graph()

In [4]: graph.bind("contributions", rdflib.Namespace("http://www.springernature.com/scigraph/things/contributions/"))

In [5]: graph.namespace_manager.normalizeUri(uri1)
Out[5]: u'contributions:b5c40759f52045a0f6e7c73befe3bd74'

In [6]: graph.namespace_manager.normalizeUri(uri2)
Out[6]: u'<http://www.springernature.com/scigraph/things/contributions/980631eef1a62ad91b437f5e50691bff>'
```

For some unexplained reason, the QName fails with `uri2` even though it's not structurally different from `uri1`.

Looking into the source for `normalizeUri`,  I can see that it relies on `split_uri` so I gave that a shot:

```
In [7]: from rdflib.namespace import split_uri

In [8]: split_uri(uri1)
Out[8]:
(u'http://www.springernature.com/scigraph/things/contributions/',
 u'b5c40759f52045a0f6e7c73befe3bd74')

In [9]: split_uri(uri2)
Out[9]:
(u'http://www.springernature.com/scigraph/things/contributions/980631',
 u'eef1a62ad91b437f5e50691bff')

```

Splitting `uri2` results in a namespace that is longer than expected (it includes the `980631` string).

Is that the cause of the error? Or am I missing something? Thanks in advance for any help.

eike...@gmail.com, 2008-02-21T21:13:03.000Z 
On Feb 21, 2008, at 1:59 PM, Philip Cooper wrote:


This is a pretty minor quibble but:

could we change Namespace.py to include a defined property title
Something like:

@property
def title(self):

title is a pretty common fragment e.g.

dc.title

issue_title: would it be possible to remove dependancy on pyreadline ?
issue_body:
indeed:
- pyreadline for Windows is a problem.
- the jupyter/ipython team replaced it by python-prompt, so there seems to be a way.

If dill is installed (with pip, conda, or plain `setup.py install`) into a Python environment, the `bin/unpickle.py` can shadow a Python module. This doesn't occur interactively, but does occur if I run another script from the `bin` directory, as Python adds the scripts dirname (in this case, the environment's `bin`) to the front of `sys.path`.

For example, suppose I do,

```
$ conda create -n test python=2.7 pytest dill
$ source activate test
$ mkdir myproject
$ cat > myproject/unpickle.py
data = "Hello, world!"
$ export PYTHONPATH=$(pwd)/myproject
$ python -c 'from unpickle import data'
```

So far so good. But if I'm running Python via some script in `bin`, for example `bin/py.test`,

```
$ mkdir myproject/tests
$ cat > myproject/tests/test_unpickle.py
from unpickle import data  # same import as interactive session
$ py.test myproject/tests/test_unpickle.py 
============================= test session starts ==============================
platform darwin -- Python 2.7.12, pytest-3.0.3, py-1.4.31, pluggy-0.4.0
rootdir: /Users/alex/tmp/dill, inifile: 
collected 0 items / 1 errors 

==================================== ERRORS ====================================
______________ ERROR collecting myproject/tests/test_unpickle.py _______________
ImportError while importing test module '/Users/alex/tmp/dill/myproject/tests/test_unpickle.py'.
Original error message:
'cannot import name data'
Make sure your test modules/packages have valid Python names.
!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!! Interrupted: 1 errors during collection !!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!
=========================== 1 error in 0.17 seconds ============================
```

This fails because under `.../bin/py.test`, the `bin` directory is in my path and dill's `unpickle.py` gets imported instead of mine.

In general, seems like a bad idea to install anything with a `.py` extension into `bin`, for this reason. Would it be possible to rename `unpickle.py` to just `unpickle`, or maybe `dill-unpickle`? Likewise for `get_objgraph.py`, though this seems less likely to collide with someone else's module.
